title: Pastoral Indicator Exceedance, Not First Appearance, Distinguishes Anthropogenic from Natural Pollen Signals: Evidence from 331 Sites in Northwestern and Central Europe
abstract: Bray-Curtis dissimilarity from pre-anthropogenic baselines is increasingly used to detect human impact on vegetation, yet dissimilarity metrics register any compositional shift without identifying its cause. We apply functional decomposition and exceedance-based classification to 331 mid-Holocene pollen sites from three European regions. Tree taxa dominate detected signals (34-57%), while pastoral and arable indicators contribute only 1-7%. We introduce a critical distinction between presence (trivially expected for native taxa) and exceedance (anomalous increase above Holocene baseline). Pastoral exceedance is observed at 87% of sites but 0% of 37 natural-phase sites. Pastoral exceedance precedes arable exceedance at 67-82% of sites, robust to exclusion of Plantago, Calluna, Poaceae, and Ulmus. The signal detects agricultural intensification (median lag 2,135 years post-Neolithic), not initial Neolithic arrival. Alternative hypotheses are systematically refuted.

novelty_statement: Formalizes the distinction between presence (uninformative for native taxa) and exceedance (anthropogenic discriminator) of pastoral indicator pollen. Demonstrates that first-appearance ordering is trivially expected (null >95%) while exceedance-based ordering provides genuine attribution evidence. Introduces a five-step protocol (Detect-Decompose-Classify-Gradient-Verify) with generalist-specialist null model, PPE-based dispersal calibration, and formal lag decomposition.
